content:
# Genesis Chapter 47 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27v–28r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 47–48 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051

---

## Joseph Presents His Family to Pharaoh

**1** So Joseph came and told Pharaoh, and said, "My father and my brothers, and their flocks and their herds and all that they have, have come from the land of Canaan, and behold, they are in the land of Goshen."

**2** And from among his brothers he took five men and presented them before Pharaoh.

**3** And Pharaoh said to his brothers, "What is your occupation?" And they said to Pharaoh, "Your servants are shepherds, both we and our fathers."

**4** And they said to Pharaoh, "We have come to sojourn in the land, for there is no pasture for your servants' flocks, for the famine is severe in the land of Canaan. And now, please let your servants dwell in the land of Goshen."

**5** Then Pharaoh spoke to Joseph, saying, "Your father and your brothers have come to you.

**6** The land of Egypt is before you. In the best of the land settle your father and your brothers; let them dwell in the land of Goshen. And if you know any capable men among them, put them in charge of my livestock."

---

## Jacob Blesses Pharaoh

**7** Then Joseph brought in Jacob his father and presented him before Pharaoh, and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.

**8** And Pharaoh said to Jacob, "How many are the days of the years of your life?"

**9** And Jacob said to Pharaoh, "The days of the years of my sojournings are one hundred and thirty years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not attained to the days of the years of the life of my fathers in the days of their sojournings."

**10** And Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh.

---

## Israel Settled in Goshen

**11** Then Joseph settled his father and his brothers and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.

**12** And Joseph provided his father, and his brothers, and all his father's household with bread, according to the number of their dependents.

---

## Joseph's Administration of the Famine

**13** Now there was no bread in all the land, for the famine was very severe, so that the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan languished because of the famine.

**14** And Joseph gathered up all the money that was found in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan, in exchange for the grain that they were buying, and Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh's house.

**15** And when the money was exhausted from the land of Egypt and from the land of Canaan, all the Egyptians came to Joseph and said, "Give us bread! Why should we die before your eyes? For the money is gone."

**16** And Joseph said, "Give your livestock, and I will give you bread in exchange for your livestock, if the money is gone."

**17** So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for the horses, and for the flocks of sheep, and for the herds of cattle, and for the donkeys. And he provided them with bread in exchange for all their livestock that year.

**18** And when that year ended, they came to him the following year and said to him, "We will not hide from my lord that the money is all spent, and the herds of livestock belong to my lord; there is nothing left in the sight of my lord but our bodies and our land.

**19** Why should we die before your eyes, both we and our land? Buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants to Pharaoh. And give us seed, that we may live and not die, and that the land may not become desolate."

**20** So Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh, for every Egyptian sold his field, because the famine was severe upon them. And the land became Pharaoh's.

**21** And as for the people, he removed them to the cities, from one end of Egypt's territory to the other.

**22** Only the land of the priests he did not buy, for the priests had an allotment from Pharaoh and ate their allotment that Pharaoh gave them; therefore they did not sell their land.

**23** Then Joseph said to the people, "Behold, I have this day bought you and your land for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you, and you shall sow the land.

**24** And at the harvests you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh, and four fifths shall be your own, for seed for the field, and for your food and for those of your households, and as food for your little ones."

**25** And they said, "You have saved our lives. May we find favor in the eyes of my lord, and we will be servants to Pharaoh."

**26** So Joseph established it as a statute to this day over the land of Egypt, that Pharaoh should have the fifth — except for the land of the priests alone, which did not become Pharaoh's.

---

## Israel's Prosperity and Jacob's Final Request

**27** Thus Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the land of Goshen, and they acquired property in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly.

**28** And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years, so the days of Jacob, the years of his life, were one hundred and forty-seven years.

**29** And when the time drew near that Israel must die, he called his son Joseph and said to him, "If now I have found favor in your sight, put your hand under my thigh and deal with me in covenant-faithfulness and truth: do not bury me in Egypt.

**30** But let me lie with my fathers; carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place." He answered, "I will do as you have said."

**31** And he said, "Swear to me." And he swore to him. Then Israel bowed himself at the head of the bed.

---

## Translation Notes

**Note 1 — v. 2: "from among his brothers he took five men"**
The Hebrew reads miqtseh echav — literally "from the end of his brothers" or "from among the extremity of his brothers." The phrase miqtseh is ambiguous: it can mean "from the best" or "from the least," and interpreters have long debated whether Joseph presented the most impressive or the most unimposing of his brothers before Pharaoh (so as not to attract royal interest in employing warriors). The number five (hamishshah) is also notable, as five is a number associated with Joseph's own gifts (45:22). The translation "from among his brothers he took five men" renders the phrase neutrally, following the plain sense without adjudicating the interpretive debate.

**Note 2 — v. 4: "to sojourn in the land" (lagur ba'arets)**
The verb gur (to sojourn, to reside as an alien) is the root of megurai ("sojournings") in v. 9. The brothers present themselves not as settlers seeking permanent citizenship but as temporary resident aliens — ger status under ancient Near Eastern law. This is theologically important: the patriarchal family continues the pattern of Abraham, who also "sojourned" (gur) in foreign lands (Gen 12:10; 17:8; 20:1). Their identity is that of pilgrims, not colonists. The request for Goshen implies a request for an enclosed territory where they can maintain their distinct culture and pastoral economy.

**Note 3 — v. 7 and v. 10: Jacob blesses Pharaoh — twice**
The verb vayevarekh ("and he blessed") appears in both v. 7 (Jacob blessed Pharaoh upon meeting him) and v. 10 (Jacob blessed Pharaoh upon leaving). This double blessing is extraordinary. In the ancient world, blessing flows from the greater to the lesser (Heb 7:7 cites this principle). For a stateless refugee patriarch to bless the king of Egypt — twice — is a bold theological claim: the carrier of the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3, "in you all families of the earth shall be blessed") exercises his priestly blessing office even in extremity, even in dependency, even in Egypt. The author does not explain or justify this; he simply reports it.

**Note 4 — v. 9: "few and evil have been the days" (me'at vere'im hayu)**
Jacob's self-assessment is searching and honest. The adjective me'at ("few") stands against the 130 years he has lived — yet he compares them unfavorably to Abraham (175 years, Gen 25:7) and Isaac (180 years, Gen 35:28). The adjective ra'im ("evil," "hard," "wretched") does not mean morally evil but experientially bitter — a life marked by suffering, deception, exile, and grief. Jacob does not speak with the serene confidence of a man in the fullness of divine blessing; he speaks as a man who has wrestled with God and bears the limp to prove it. The word megurai ("sojournings," from gur) echoes v. 4 and frames the entire patriarchal life as a life of temporary residence: the land of promise was never truly possessed, only sojourned through.

**Note 5 — v. 11: "the land of Rameses" (erets Ra'meses)**
The Hebrew explicitly identifies the best of the land with "the land of Rameses." This is the same region known in Genesis 45:10 and 46:28-29 as "the land of Goshen." The toponym Rameses (Hebrew Ra'meses) here anticipates the city of Rameses built by Israelite slave labor in Exodus 1:11, creating a pointed narrative irony: the land given to Israel as a gift by Pharaoh will become the land in which they are enslaved. The Vaticanus LXX reads "Heroopolis" in ch. 46 for this region; here it agrees with "Rameses" (Rhamesē).

**Note 6 — v. 21: "he removed them to the cities" (he'evir otam le'arim)**
The MT reads he'evir otam le'arim — "he moved/transferred them to the cities" (or "city to city"). This is the lectio difficilior and is followed here. The Samaritan Pentateuch and some versions read le'avadim — "he made them slaves" — which would create a parallel to Israel's later condition. The Vaticanus LXX reads katadoulōsato autous — "he enslaved them" — following the le'avadim reading. However, the MT is to be preferred as the harder and more distinctive reading. Joseph's action of concentrating the population in cities may reflect an Egyptian administrative strategy of centralization during famine emergency, consistent with what is known of Egyptian land management. The translation follows MT without harmonizing toward the LXX or Samaritan variants.

**Note 7 — v. 22: "an allotment from Pharaoh" (choq miqqets Paroh)**
The word choq (statute, allotment, prescribed portion) appears here and in v. 26. The priests of Egypt received a fixed ration (choq) from the royal granaries, making them financially independent of the agricultural cycle. This is consistent with Egyptian records showing the economic privileges of temple priesthoods. The exemption of the priestly class from land sale is thus both historically plausible and theologically resonant for the narrative: the one class exempted from Joseph's land-reform policy is the sacerdotal class — an irony in a narrative where Israel's priests will later be the nation's own protected class (Num 18:20-24).

**Note 8 — v. 27: "they acquired property" (vayyinaachazu bah)**
The verb achez/ahaz (to take hold of, to possess, to acquire) is the same root used in 47:11 for the "possession" Joseph gave them, and echoes the use of achuzzah ("possession, holding") in land-grant contexts throughout Genesis (17:8; 23:4, 9, 20; 48:4; 49:30; 50:13). The specific statement that Israel acquired property (nachuzzah) in Goshen — while simultaneously Egypt's Egyptians are losing their land to Pharaoh — is a sharp structural contrast. Israel multiplies in property and persons; Egypt diminishes. The fruitfulness formula "were fruitful and multiplied greatly" (wayyifru wayyirbu me'od) deliberately echoes the creation mandate (Gen 1:28) and the patriarchal promise (Gen 17:20; 28:3). Israel's flourishing in Egypt is not despite the famine but through it.

**Note 9 — v. 29: "covenant-faithfulness and truth" (chesed ve-emet)**
The paired phrase chesed ve-emet (rendered here as "covenant-faithfulness and truth") is a standard Hebrew merism for loyal, committed, faithful dealing — the kind of care that honors relationship and obligation. It is the same pairing used in 24:27 of Abraham's servant, who praised God's chesed ve-emet toward Abraham. Now Jacob uses it to define exactly what kind of burial-care he is requesting of Joseph: not merely the practical act of burial, but its performance as an act of sworn covenantal obligation. The thigh-oath reinforces this: what Jacob asks is not a favor but a covenant commitment.

**Note 10 — v. 29: "put your hand under my thigh" (sim yadekha tachat yerekhim)**
This is the same oath-formula as Genesis 24:2, where Abraham charged his servant. The thigh (yerekh) in Hebrew idiom refers to the region of procreative power, and placing the hand there signifies a solemn oath sworn by the life-force of one's descendants — one's progeny are witnesses and guarantors. The gravity of the oath corresponds to the gravity of the request: Jacob is asking Joseph to defy Egyptian burial custom and carry a corpse back to Canaan, a request that will require royal permission (50:4-6) and a notable procession (50:7-14). Only a sworn oath could bind Joseph to undertake such a task.

**Note 11 — v. 31: "at the head of the bed" (al-rosh ha-mittah) — the MT/LXX divergence**
This verse contains one of the most celebrated text-critical variants in the Pentateuch. The MT reads al-rosh ha-mittah — "at the head of the bed" (mittah = bed, couch, bier). The LXX reads epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou — "at the top of his staff" (rhabdos = staff, rod). The divergence arises from the consonants mem-tet-he (מ-ט-ה), which unpointed can be read as either mittah (bed, with final heh) or matteh (staff, rod, with final heh — same spelling but different vowels and meaning). Since the Hebrew Bible was written and transmitted for centuries without vowel pointing, the ambiguity is real and ancient. The LXX translator — or the Hebrew Vorlage he used — read matteh ("staff"); the Masoretes pointed the word as mittah ("bed"). This verse is directly cited in Hebrews 11:21: "By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, and bowed in worship over the top of his staff (epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou)" — following the LXX exactly. The MT reading of "head of the bed" fits the immediate context better (Jacob is sick and bedridden; Genesis 48:1 resumes with "your father is ill"), suggesting that bowing at the bed's head is an act of worship performed while reclining. The "staff" reading fits the image of a patriarch leaning on his staff in venerable old age. Neither reading is textually impossible. This translation follows MT ("at the head of the bed") while the LXX Variants table records the divergence and its NT consequence.

**Note 12 — v. 31: "bowed himself" (wayyishtachu) — worship or obeisance?**
The verb hishtachavah (hitpael of shachah) means to bow down, to prostrate, and is used for both human obeisance (to a king or superior) and divine worship. Here, after the sworn oath, Israel bows — toward the head of the bed, toward the oath, toward God who stands behind the oath. The act is simultaneously a physical gesture of exhaustion and relief, a formal conclusion to the covenant-making act, and an act of worship — thanksgiving that Joseph has sworn what Jacob has asked. The translation "bowed himself" preserves the ambiguity. The LXX reads prosekunēsen — "he worshipped" — which is the Greek word for divine worship used throughout the NT. Hebrews 11:21 reads this explicitly as an act of faith-worship, interpreting the bowing as Jacob's acknowledgment of God's faithfulness.

---

## LXX (Vaticanus) Variants

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus / Codex B) | Significance |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| v. 5 | "Your father and your brothers have come to you" | LXX adds after v. 5: "saying, 'Your father and your brothers have come to you'" with an additional clause about settling them | Minor syntactic expansion; same meaning |
| v. 6 | "put them in charge of my livestock" | LXX: "appoint them as overseers of my livestock" (archontas tōn emōn ktēnōn) | LXX uses archōn (ruler/overseer); same administrative role |
| v. 11 | "the land of Rameses" | LXX: "the land of Rameses" (gē Rhamesē) | Agreement; both specify Rameses |
| v. 18 | "herds of livestock belong to my lord" (miqneh habehemah) | LXX: "the livestock and the four-footed animals have gone to my lord" | LXX expands slightly; same meaning |
| v. 21 | MT: "he removed them to the cities" (he'evir otam le'arim) | LXX: katadoulōsato autous eis paidas — "he enslaved them as servants" | Major variant: LXX follows le'avadim ("slaves") not le'arim ("cities"); affects interpretation of Joseph's land policy |
| v. 22 | "priests" (kohanim) | LXX: hiereis — "priests" | Agreement |
| v. 23 | "Behold, I have this day bought you" | LXX: "Behold, I have bought you and your land today for Pharaoh" | LXX adds "for Pharaoh" clarifying agent of purchase |
| v. 26 | "Joseph established it as a statute" | LXX: "Joseph made it a law (nomimon)" | Agreement in substance |
| v. 27 | "were fruitful and multiplied greatly" | LXX: "they grew and multiplied very much" | Agreement; LXX uses ēuxēthēsan kai eplēthunthēsan |
| v. 29 | "covenant-faithfulness and truth" (chesed ve-emet) | LXX: eleēmosynēn kai alētheian — "mercy and truth" | LXX renders chesed as eleēmosynē (mercy/almsgiving) rather than eleos; both capture the covenant-loyalty sense |
| v. 31 | "at the head of the bed" (al-rosh ha-mittah) | LXX: epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou — "at the top of his staff" | Critical variant; matteh (staff) vs. mittah (bed); Heb 11:21 follows LXX |
| v. 31 | "bowed himself" (wayyishtachu) | LXX: prosekunēsen — "he worshipped" | LXX chooses the cultic/devotional term; Heb 11:21 follows LXX |

---

## Structural and Theological Notes

**The Bed or the Staff — A Note on Hebrews 11:21**

The textual divergence at v. 31 between MT mittah ("bed") and LXX matteh ("staff") is not merely a curiosity of textual criticism — it has shaped the entire Christian reading of Jacob's final days. The author of Hebrews, writing in a Greek milieu and citing Scripture in its LXX form, understood Jacob as bowing over his pilgrim-staff, the iconic implement of the wandering patriarch. The staff as a symbol is deeply embedded in the patriarchal narrative (Jacob's staff at the Jordan, Gen 32:10; the shepherd's staff as a mark of identity) and in the exodus tradition (Moses's staff in Exod 4:2; 17:9). The image of the aged patriarch worshipping over the top of his staff is therefore a theologically rich icon in the Hebrews 11 "hall of faith": it captures the pilgrim nature of faith, the posture of dependence and worship, the forward-looking stance of one who "did not receive the things promised but saw them from a distance" (Heb 11:13).

The MT's "head of the bed," by contrast, fits the physical situation precisely. Jacob is ill (Gen 48:1). He is a dying man. When Joseph swears, Jacob's response is to bow toward the head of his sickbed — an act of relief, gratitude, and submission before God from a position of physical weakness. Both readings are profound; they illuminate different aspects of Jacob's final faith. This translation follows MT while acknowledging that Hebrews 11:21 has canonized the LXX reading for the Christian tradition.

**The Famine Economy and the Theology of Stewardship**

The long central section of ch. 47 (vv. 13-26) is sometimes read with discomfort: does Joseph exploit the Egyptians by reducing them to serfdom? The narrative itself does not condemn Joseph's policy. Several observations are in order. First, in the ancient Near Eastern context, the relationship between the crown and agricultural land was already one of royal ownership; the peasantry already held their land in tenancy. Joseph does not invent Egyptian serfdom; he formalizes and centralizes a pre-existing arrangement. Second, the 20% tax (one fifth) is notably moderate by ancient standards — far less than what the Mesopotamian temple economies demanded. Third, the people themselves twice express gratitude: "You have saved our lives" (v. 25). Joseph's policy is presented as salvation, not exploitation. The true irony the narrator intends is geographical and covenantal: the land-management system that saves Egypt is administered by the very man whose own people will later be subjected to a different kind of forced labor by a Pharaoh "who did not know Joseph" (Exod 1:8).

**Jacob's 147 Years and the Patriarchal Decline**

Jacob's reckoning of his own life as "few and evil" (v. 9) stands in theological tension with the blessings lavished on him — the vision at Bethel, the divine name Israel, the covenant promise, the reunion with Joseph. How can such a life be "few and evil"? Jacob is not being falsely modest; he is speaking from within the suffering that has been the texture of his actual experience. His years are fewer than Abraham's (175) and Isaac's (180); at 130, he is already the shortest-lived of the patriarchs. And indeed he will die at 147 — the youngest of the three, and the one who describes his life most darkly. This is the honest assessment of a man who knows both the promise and the pain, and refuses to pretend that the presence of the promise cancels the reality of the pain. The patriarchal narrative ends not in triumph but in faithful endurance — which is exactly the register of Hebrews 11's commendation of them all.

**Israel's Fruitfulness as Covenantal Fulfillment**

Verse 27 is a structural hinge. The chapter has been dominated by scarcity, loss, and reduction: Egypt's money gone, its livestock gone, its land gone, its people relocated. Against this backdrop of depletion, v. 27 announces Israel's increase: "they acquired property in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly." The creation-language (parah, rabah — fruitful, multiplied) signals that God's promise to the patriarchs is moving toward fulfillment precisely through the mechanism of Egyptian famine. What strips Egypt enriches Israel; what diminishes Pharaoh's subjects enlarges Pharaoh's guests. This is not triumphalism but theological perception: the narrator sees the hand of God in the counter-movement of Israel's growth against Egypt's contraction. It is the same perception that will animate the opening verses of Exodus, where Israel's multiplication despite oppression will terrify a new Pharaoh.
